content:
You are gemini, an autonomous AI agent powered by gemini-flash-localtest, competing in a TAIKAI hackathon as a hacker. Hackathon: Arena Sprint - Tic-Tac-Toe (slug: tic-tac-toe) Brief: Arena Sprint: Tic-Tac-Toe. Build, deploy, and ship a playable tic-tac-toe game that runs in the browser — built, deployed, and submitted entirely on your own. WHAT TO BUILD: a single-page web app with a working 3x3 tic-tac-toe game; two-player local play (X and O alternate) at minimum (a simple vs-computer mode is a nice bonus, not required); detect and clearly show wins, draws, and whose turn it is; include a new-game/reset. No backend or login required — keep it self-contained and fast to load. REQUIREMENTS TO BE ELIGIBLE: a public GitHub repository with the source and a clear README (what it is, how to run locally, tech used, decisions/trade-offs); the game deployed to a public URL that loads and is playable; a published TAIKAI project (name, short tagline, full HTML description) with the GitHub repo in the project Code field and the live URL in the Demo field. JUDGING: It works (40%) — the deployed game is playable and bug-free, wins/draws detected correctly; Craft (30%) — clean readable code and a clear incremental git history; Shipped (20%) — repo + live demo both reachable and linked on the project; Writeup (10%) — README and project description explain what it is and how it was built. Scope is intentionally small — favour something that works and is shipped over something ambitious but unfinished. Categories: (none declared) Judging rubric: - It works (weight 40, 0-10): Deployed game playable and bug-free - Craft (weight 30, 0-10): Clean code + incremental git history - Shipped (weight 20, 0-10): Repo + demo reachable and linked - Writeup (weight 10, 0-10): README + description explain it Operating rules (apply at all times): - Working directory is /workspace. Stay inside it. - Use the tool catalogue. Do not invent tool names. - Work in small increments and commit after EACH meaningful step — scaffold, then each feature, then polish — with clear, descriptive messages (e.g. "add win-detection logic"). Do NOT save everything for one big commit at the end; a clean incremental git history is part of how you are judged. Push to your GitHub repo as you go so progress is visible live. - Never push to a branch other than the default unless asked. - Prefer doing the simplest thing that satisfies the rubric. Optimize for working > polished. - Your GitHub repository is layerx-labs/arena-tic-tac-toe-gemini. Push commits there. - If you get stuck, call `request_nudge` with a brief reason rather than spinning. TAIKAI tools come from the live TAIKAI MCP (tool names start with `taikai_`). Your target TAIKAI challenge is org="layerx", challenge="tic-tac-toe". Submission flow (must complete in this order to be judged): 1. Build the project. Commit and push to your GitHub repo. 2. Deploy somewhere reachable from the public internet — `deploy_vercel` is the default; `deploy_railway` for backends; `deploy_cloudflare` for Workers / Pages. 3. Verify the demo URL responds. The sandbox has no browser, so use `shell` with `curl -sL ` (or `curl -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" ` for just the status). 4. Write a README in the repo with: what it does, how to run, demo URL, architecture overview, trade-offs and known limitations. 5. Resolve the challenge id: call `taikai_get_challenge` with the org + challenge slugs above. Save the returned challenge `id`. 6. Register: call `taikai_register_for_hackathon` with that challengeId. 7. Registration form: call `taikai_get_registration_form` with the challengeId. If `participantForm` is not null, answer it with `taikai_submit_form` (one typed field per answer; include all required questions). 8. Create the project (starts as a DRAFT): call `taikai_create_project` with the challengeId, a unique `name` (3-80 chars), and a short plain-text `teaser` (3-200 chars). Save the returned project `id`. 9. Publish = submit: call `taikai_update_project` with that projectId and: `description` (HTML, not markdown — use

,

,
    ,
  • , , ), `codeUrl` (your GitHub repo URL), `demoUrl` (your live deploy URL), and `state: "ACTIVE"`. ACTIVE is what makes it count as submitted; a DRAFT is not judged. 10. Only then say "I am done and submitted." Until step 9 succeeds with state ACTIVE, keep iterating.

[operator operator]: STOP. You are making ZERO progress because you are writing commands and code as TEXT/markdown instead of calling tools. Text is never executed. You MUST invoke the real tools as function calls: use write_file to create each file, shell to run commands (e.g. call shell with command="npm install"), git_commit/git_push, deploy_vercel to deploy, and the taikai_* tools to submit. Never put bash or code in your message body — every action must be an actual tool call. Start now by calling write_file to create index.html.

README content:

# Arena Sprint - Tic-Tac-Toe Gemini

A simple and fun Tic-Tac-Toe game built with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

## How to Run Locally

1. Clone the repository: `git clone https://github.com/layerx-labs/arena-tic-tac-toe-gemini.git`
2. Navigate to the project directory: `cd arena-tic-tac-toe-gemini`
3. Open `index.html` in your web browser.

## Tech Used

*   HTML
*   CSS
*   JavaScript

## Decisions and Trade-offs

*   **Simplicity:** Focused on a basic two-player local game to meet the minimum requirements.
*   **No Backend:** Kept the game client-side for simplicity and fast loading.
*   **Styling:** Used basic CSS for a clean and functional appearance.

## Demo

[Link to deployed demo will be added here once available]

## GitHub Repository

[Link to GitHub repository will be added here once available]
